title: Consumption Risk-Sharing in Social Networks
abstract: We develop a model in which connections between individuals serve as social collateral to enforce informal insurance payments. We show that: (i) The degree of insurance is governed by the expansiveness of the network, measured with the per capita number of connections that groups have with the rest of the community. “Two-dimensional” networks—like real-world networks in Peruvian villages—are sufficiently expansive to allow very good risk-sharing. (ii) In second-best arrangements, insurance is local: agents fully share shocks within, but imperfectly between endogenously emerging risk-sharing groups. We also discuss how endogenous social collateral affects our results. (JEL D85, G22, O15, O17, Z13)
summary_en: Rural households without formal insurance rely on each other for help during shocks. This paper asks: what makes such informal insurance work? The answer: the structure of the social network. "Two-dimensional" networks — like those documented in Peruvian villages, where people have many overlapping ties — offer enough expansiveness to sustain very good risk-sharing. But the insurance is "local": shocks are fully shared within endogenously-formed groups, only imperfectly between them. The paper shows how the community's structure of relationships functions as social collateral that enforces informal-insurance commitments, and how that structure determines equilibrium welfare.
data_used: Theoretical model of informal risk-sharing, illustrated with social-network patterns documented in Peruvian villages.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian policy, the paper speaks most directly to rural-development and poverty research: interventions that accidentally disrupt community network structures (migration programs, housing relocation) can undermine informal insurance, hurting vulnerable households. Relevant to KRTK rural and poverty research. Caveat: the main empirical illustration is from Peru; Hungarian rural communities have both formal social-protection systems and different network structures, so the magnitudes won't translate directly.
